jokes.text: You're entering the vicinity of an area adjacent to a location. A place you might find a monster or Weird mirror.
jokes.setup: Parody of Twilight Zone opening with overly verbose, redundant description
jokes.punchline: The completely unnecessary wordiness of 'vicinity of an area adjacent to a location'
jokes.explanation: Excellent parody writing that perfectly captures and exaggerates the pretentious tone of Twilight Zone while being hilariously redundant

jokes.text: Wait, my eyes aren't that bad. I can read the large print books.
jokes.setup: Classic Twilight Zone episode where last man's glasses break
jokes.punchline: Character finds optimistic loophole in his tragic situation
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of expectations from the classic episode, showing character's resilience

jokes.text: It's not...! Well, lucky I can read braille.
jokes.setup: Large print books are destroyed too
jokes.punchline: Another optimistic pivot to braille as final solution
jokes.explanation: Continues the escalation effectively, though the pattern is becoming predictable

jokes.text: Hey, look, a weird mirror. Cursed by his own hubris.
jokes.setup: Dramatic moment of character presumably losing his hands
jokes.punchline: Immediate cut to casual observation about a weird mirror and deadpan moral
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing and tonal whiplash from tragedy to mundane observation

jokes.text: So? We live in the United States. That's part of the world. I have been gone a long time.
jokes.setup: Learning about world president affecting the US
jokes.punchline: Fry's delayed realization of how much the world has changed
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Fry's fish-out-of-water status and delayed processing

jokes.text: But frankly, I agree with everything he just said!
jokes.setup: Candidate claiming to respect his opponent
jokes.punchline: Completely agreeing with everything, showing no actual difference
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire about candidates who have no real differences. Well-structured punchline.

jokes.text: These candidates sound like clones. Wait, they are clones!
jokes.setup: Fry making an observation about similar-sounding candidates
jokes.punchline: The literal reveal that they actually are clones
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that takes a metaphor and makes it literal. Classic sci-fi comedy structure.

jokes.text: Your three-cent titanium tax goes too far! Your three-cent titanium tax doesn't go too far enough!
jokes.setup: Two candidates supposedly differing on key issues
jokes.punchline: Their 'difference' is completely meaningless semantic hair-splitting
jokes.explanation: Brilliant political satire about meaningless partisan differences, perfectly structured with grammatically nonsensical positions

jokes.text: If I were registered, I'd send a message by staying home on election day, dressed like a clown.
jokes.setup: Fry wanting to send a political message
jokes.punchline: The completely illogical plan involving clown costume for an action no one would see
jokes.explanation: Pure Fry logic - the clown costume detail makes an already illogical plan completely absurd

jokes.text: You're not registered? Not vaccinated, either.
jokes.setup: Surprise about not being registered to vote
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation of being completely disengaged from civic responsibilities
jokes.explanation: Great character consistency - Fry's casual admission reveals his complete lack of adult responsibility

jokes.text: John Quincy Adding Machine. He enticed voters by pledging not to go on a killing spree.
jokes.setup: Historical reference to first robot president
jokes.punchline: Robot name parody and hilariously low bar for presidential qualifications
jokes.explanation: Clever historical parody with perfect robot naming convention and dark humor about low expectations

jokes.text: But, like most, he promised more than he could deliver.
jokes.setup: Robot president promising not to kill
jokes.punchline: Implication he went on killing sprees anyway, delivered as dry political commentary
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery of dark implication, treating mass murder as typical political disappointment

jokes.text: When did you care about voting? The very instant I became old.
jokes.setup: Question about sudden interest in voting
jokes.punchline: Farnsworth's brutally honest admission about aging and political engagement
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational humor about how aging changes political priorities, perfectly fits Farnsworth's character

jokes.text: The problem is, both parties give your tax dollars... to the less fortunate. They get all the breaks!
jokes.setup: Nixon complaining about both political parties
jokes.punchline: His twisted logic that poor people 'get all the breaks'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Nixon characterization - completely backwards logic delivered with conviction. Good political satire.

jokes.text: I'd better keep an eye out at the next meeting.
jokes.setup: Statement about weirdos and mutants joining third parties
jokes.punchline: Leela, who is herself a mutant, planning to watch for them
jokes.explanation: Nice character-based irony as Leela doesn't realize she fits the description herself

jokes.text: What's your party, Bender? I can't vote. Because you're a robot? No, a felon.
jokes.setup: Assumption that robots can't vote
jokes.punchline: Bender's criminal record is the actual disqualification
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection that subverts expectations while being completely consistent with Bender's criminal character

jokes.text: Attach Brain Slugs to them. Sure, you say that now.
jokes.setup: Question about what Brain Slugs will do for working man
jokes.punchline: Horrifying answer delivered matter-of-factly, Fry's skeptical response as if it's a typical campaign promise
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor treating mind control as just another political platform, with Fry's response being perfectly blasé

jokes.text: Dave's not here, man.
jokes.setup: Question about hemp products
jokes.punchline: Classic stoner reference from Cheech and Chong routine
jokes.explanation: Lazy reference humor that relies on recognition rather than original wit, though it fits the stoner party theme

jokes.text: I don't go anywhere without my mutated anthrax for duck hunting.
jokes.setup: Discussion of doomsday devices for mad scientists
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of using biological weapons for mundane hunting
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor juxtaposing weapons of mass destruction with recreational hunting

jokes.text: Sure, humans are cute, but how will we test cosmetics? I concur.
jokes.setup: Casual conversation between robots
jokes.punchline: Treating human experimentation as a practical necessity for cosmetics testing
jokes.explanation: Decent dark humor showing robots' casual disregard for human welfare, though not particularly original

jokes.text: Not with that attitude. Okay, screw it. Welcome aboard, brother! All right! You're out.
jokes.setup: Fry trying to join a party
jokes.punchline: Rapid-fire acceptance and rejection based on attitude changes
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing the arbitrary and fickle nature of political party membership

jokes.text: Jack Johnson, or bitter rival, John Jackson?
jokes.setup: Announcing the two presidential candidates
jokes.punchline: The names are nearly identical, showing no real choice
jokes.explanation: Good political commentary through name similarity. Shows the illusion of choice in elections.

jokes.text: Terrific candidates, huh, Morbo? All humans are vermin in the eyes of Morbo.
jokes.setup: Standard news anchor asking for co-host's opinion
jokes.punchline: Morbo's consistent misanthropist character responding honestly
jokes.explanation: Perfect character consistency for Morbo, treating genocidal thoughts as normal news commentary

jokes.text: Without a quick response, the robots will die within 300 years.
jokes.setup: Urgent news about trapped robots needing rescue
jokes.punchline: The absurdly long timeline presented as urgent
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist humor subverting emergency news conventions with robot longevity

jokes.text: We'll pave over the area, then move on.
jokes.setup: Question about rescue operations for trapped robots
jokes.punchline: Callously practical non-solution delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Efficiently dark humor about bureaucratic callousness and cover-up mentality

jokes.text: I'm finally richer than those snooty ATMs.
jokes.setup: Bender learning his body is valuable due to titanium content
jokes.punchline: Comparing wealth to ATMs as if they're a social class
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bender logic treating machines as social rivals, creative and character-specific

jokes.text: Too bad you can't spend it. Oh, can't I? No. Watch me, poor man.
jokes.setup: Leela pointing out Bender can't spend his body
jokes.punchline: Bender's defiant response and calling her 'poor man'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bender's stubborn determination and casual insults. Sets up his body-selling plan.

jokes.text: Compare your lives to mine, and then kill yourselves.
jokes.setup: Bender gloating about his newfound wealth
jokes.punchline: Casual suggestion of suicide as logical response to envy
jokes.explanation: Classic Bender callousness delivered with perfect casual cruelty, highly quotable

jokes.text: You just lost five dollars.
jokes.setup: Offering five dollars to not do something violent
jokes.punchline: Immediate consequence for the violent action anyway
jokes.explanation: Well-structured setup and payoff showing Bender's cruel but logical approach to transactions

jokes.text: Sold your body? Bender, I've been down that road. It's glamorous, the parties are great, but you'll spend every dollar on jewelry and skintight pants.
jokes.setup: Zoidberg offering advice about selling one's body
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's talking about prostitution while Bender meant literal body parts
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection humor with perfect character moment for Zoidberg's seedy past

jokes.text: Bodies are for hookers and fat people.
jokes.setup: Question about living without a body
jokes.punchline: Bender's dismissive categorization of who needs bodies
jokes.explanation: Peak Bender attitude with perfectly economical dismissal of physical existence

jokes.text: What's he gonna do, break my legs?
jokes.setup: Mention of loan shark threatening Bender
jokes.punchline: Bender realizes he no longer has legs to break
jokes.explanation: Decent situational humor playing on Bender's current state as just a head

jokes.text: 21 red. I beg your pardon, 33 black. Yes! The rich get richer!
jokes.setup: Bender gambling at roulette
jokes.punchline: Croupier 'corrects' the result in Bender's favor, Bender acts like it's natural
jokes.explanation: Decent sight gag showing corruption favoring the wealthy, with Bender's oblivious acceptance

jokes.text: Can I ride in your car? No, it looks like your neck stepped in something. Oh, I'm sorry, that's just your body.
jokes.setup: Bender asking for a ride, getting rejected for appearance
jokes.punchline: Insult about neck appearance turns out to be about having a body
jokes.explanation: Good setup and revelation, playing on class differences between heads and bodied people

jokes.text: Can I ride in your car? No, it looks like your neck stepped in something. Oh, I'm sorry, that's just your body.
jokes.setup: Bender asking for a ride from a rich person
jokes.punchline: The insult about his neck appearance, then realizing it's his missing body
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag that highlights Bender's current predicament. The rich person's casual cruelty fits the theme.

jokes.text: Look me in the shins and say that!
jokes.setup: Traditional 'look me in the eye' confrontation setup
jokes.punchline: Bender adapts the phrase to his current head-only status
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that shows Bender adapting idioms to his physical situation

jokes.text: I'm off to hang out with classy heads like me, who appreciate poetry, philosophy, hats.
jokes.setup: Bender leaving to find more sophisticated company
jokes.punchline: The anticlimatic inclusion of 'hats' with poetry and philosophy
jokes.explanation: Good rule of three with unexpected mundane item, fits Bender's pretentious but shallow character

jokes.text: Could one of you coffin stuffers please carry me?
jokes.setup: Bender just insulted his friends as 'coffin stuffers'
jokes.punchline: Immediately needs their help and asks politely
jokes.explanation: Good immediate comeuppance for Bender's arrogance, showing his practical needs

jokes.text: I'd like to lose a few pounds. Couldn't hurt.
jokes.setup: Supermodel's head talking about losing weight
jokes.punchline: Bender agreeing that a disembodied head could lose weight
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor about body image issues applying to heads only

jokes.text: Legally, nothing I can do counts as sex anymore.
jokes.setup: Clinton's head being inappropriate around women
jokes.punchline: Technical loophole about physical limitations of being just a head
jokes.explanation: Perfect character reference to Clinton's scandals with clever legal technicality twist

jokes.text: I got trashed on Listerine.
jokes.setup: Fry explaining why he didn't vote
jokes.punchline: Using mouthwash as alcohol substitute
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing Fry's resourcefulness and poor judgment

jokes.text: I've never felt voting is essential to the process.
jokes.setup: Former president discussing voting
jokes.punchline: Complete dismissal of democratic participation by someone who benefited from it
jokes.explanation: Good political satire about politicians' disconnect from democratic process

jokes.text: That's not my expansion slot and my friend says, That's not my 25-pin connector!
jokes.setup: Bender telling a robot joke to the presidents
jokes.punchline: Computer hardware innuendo replacing sexual terms
jokes.explanation: Decent tech humor playing on robot sexuality with computer hardware terms

jokes.text: Bender, thou robots really cracketh me up.
jokes.setup: Shakespeare responding to Bender's joke
jokes.punchline: Using Elizabethan English to discuss modern robot humor
jokes.explanation: Decent character consistency with Shakespeare's speech patterns in modern context

jokes.text: No offense, Jefferson, Nixon and Truman.
jokes.setup: Saying can't let every Tom, Dick and Harry move in
jokes.punchline: Realizes the names correspond to actual presidents present
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay connecting common expression to actual presidential names

jokes.text: LaRouche won't stop telling jokes.
jokes.setup: Explaining why closet of presidential losers is undesirable
jokes.punchline: Lyndon LaRouche characterized as annoyingly joke-obsessed
jokes.explanation: Decent character detail for obscure political figure, though somewhat inside baseball

jokes.text: Same reason you hocked your teeth. Ah, booze money.
jokes.setup: Washington asking why Bender sold his body
jokes.punchline: Revealing Washington also made desperate sales for alcohol
jokes.explanation: Good character revelation showing founding fathers had similar vices

jokes.text: A good Republican body. God, I loved it.
jokes.setup: Nixon describing his old body
jokes.punchline: Characterizing physical attributes as politically aligned
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Nixon's tendency to politicize everything

jokes.text: He opened relations with China. He doesn't care about your ding-dong.
jokes.setup: Nixon talking about his teen years with his body
jokes.punchline: Dismissive response focusing on his political achievements vs. personal topics
jokes.explanation: Decent deflection humor contrasting political legacy with personal matters

jokes.text: That's my style. Kick them when they're down.
jokes.setup: Nixon giving bad news after someone is already disappointed
jokes.punchline: Self-aware admission of his cruel timing
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency showing Nixon's self-awareness about his mean nature

jokes.text: What an awful dream. Ones and zeros everywhere! And I thought I saw a two.
jokes.setup: Bender describing a nightmare
jokes.punchline: His robot-specific fears about binary code and impossible numbers
jokes.explanation: Brilliant robot-specific nightmare that makes perfect sense for Bender. The 'two' in binary is genuinely scary for a robot. Highly quotable and original.

jokes.text: It was just a dream. There are no twos.
jokes.setup: Bender having nightmare about seeing a two in binary
jokes.punchline: Leela's reassurance treating binary limitations as comforting
jokes.explanation: Creative tech humor about binary nightmares with perfect comfort response

jokes.text: It was just a dream. There are no twos.
jokes.setup: Fry comforting Bender about his nightmare
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact confirmation about binary mathematics
jokes.explanation: Perfect follow-up that treats Bender's robot fears as completely rational. Fry's casual understanding is sweet and funny.

jokes.text: You sold my body! To who? I can't reveal that. But I'll give you $50 for the kid.
jokes.setup: Bender discovering his body was sold
jokes.punchline: The pawn shop owner immediately trying to buy a child
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation showing the pawn shop owner's criminal nature. The casual child trafficking offer is appropriately disturbing.

jokes.text: What about your self-destruct button? It's on my body!
jokes.setup: Suggestion that Bender could self-destruct to end his misery
jokes.punchline: His means of suicide is attached to the body he sold
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor setup with perfect logical problem for Bender's situation

jokes.text: That's right, no 'body.' But, as you can plainly see, I've got a shiny new body.
jokes.setup: Constitutional rule about nobody being president more than twice
jokes.punchline: Nixon exploiting the literal meaning of 'nobody' since he now has a different body
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that's both legally clever and completely in character for Nixon's scheming nature

jokes.text: That double-crossing bastard! How dare he run off with Nixon!
jokes.setup: Bender angry about his body being stolen
jokes.punchline: More upset about Nixon than the theft itself
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bender's misplaced priorities and personal grievances

jokes.text: But when a robotic Nixon is loose, we have to take action! Idiot.
jokes.setup: Leela explaining why they need to help despite Bender being an idiot
jokes.punchline: Still calls him an idiot after agreeing to help
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency showing Leela's practical helpfulness and honest assessment

jokes.text: Belligerent and numerous.
jokes.setup: Nixon asking about Morbo's family
jokes.punchline: Morbo's characteristically threatening description of his family
jokes.explanation: Perfect Morbo character moment with menacing family description delivered casually

jokes.text: The question is vague. You don't say what kind of candy, whether anyone is watching... I certainly wouldn't harm the child.
jokes.setup: Nixon responding to the candy question under a lie detector
jokes.punchline: His lawyerly evasion and specific denial about harming the child (implying he'd take the candy)
jokes.explanation: Perfect Nixon characterization - finding loopholes while technically not lying. The careful wording reveals his true intentions.

jokes.text: I certainly wouldn't harm the child.
jokes.setup: Nixon under truth-o-scope answering about taking candy from child
jokes.punchline: His clearly dishonest answer while the truth detector presumably shows otherwise
jokes.explanation: Decent visual gag playing on Nixon's reputation for dishonesty

jokes.text: But your body's message was, 'Look at my shiny new body.' You've got real charisma from the neck down.
jokes.setup: Nixon thinking he did poorly in debate
jokes.punchline: His body language was more important than his actual responses
jokes.explanation: Good political satire about image over substance with clever 'neck down' phrasing

jokes.text: Shut up! Seriously, I'm never giving back this body. Beat it! Or I'll get Cambodian on your asses!
jokes.setup: Confrontation about returning Bender's body
jokes.punchline: Nixon's threat references his historical bombing of Cambodia
jokes.explanation: Excellent historical reference delivered as casual threat, perfectly fits Nixon's character

jokes.text: Not for five cents, not for 500 cents.
jokes.setup: Refusing to recycle Bender's head for deposit
jokes.punchline: Fry's mathematical incompetence shown in his dramatic declaration
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Fry's loyalty and mathematical ignorance

jokes.text: We're in a room! Then lose some weight!
jokes.setup: Suggestion to 'get a room' for intimate behavior
jokes.punchline: Literal response followed by weight-based insult
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation but fairly generic fat joke without much character specificity

jokes.text: The voters aren't pea-brained idiots anymore. No? Listen here, missy.
jokes.setup: Leela arguing that voters are smarter now
jokes.punchline: Nixon's dismissive response and sexist address
jokes.explanation: Sets up Nixon's cynical speech about voters. 'Missy' is perfectly in character for his condescending nature.

jokes.text: Computers may be twice as fast as they were in 1973, but voters are as drunk and stupid as ever!
jokes.setup: Response to claim that voters aren't pea-brained idiots anymore
jokes.punchline: Nixon's cynical assessment of technological progress vs. human nature
jokes.explanation: Excellent political cynicism that rings true, perfectly delivered by Nixon character

jokes.text: Once I'm in office, I'll sell our children's organs to zoos for meat! I'll go into people's houses at night and wreck up the place!
jokes.setup: Nixon explaining how he's become bitter and crazy
jokes.punchline: Increasingly unhinged campaign promises that are completely counterproductive
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation showing Nixon's complete break from reality with memorable absurd promises

jokes.text: I'll go into people's houses at night and wreck up the place!
jokes.setup: Nixon continuing his horrible campaign promises
jokes.punchline: Promising random home vandalism as presidential policy
jokes.explanation: Great escalation that's both petty and presidential. 'Wreck up the place' is perfectly casual for such a terrible promise.

jokes.text: He lost my vote. One vote won't make a difference.
jokes.setup: Someone being turned off by Nixon's promises
jokes.punchline: Fry's dismissal of voting importance
jokes.explanation: Sets up the episode's voting theme. Fry's apathy is character-consistent but this line is mainly functional.

jokes.text: I thought I had a Clark Gable quality.
jokes.setup: Nixon hearing his voice on tape
jokes.punchline: Delusional self-assessment of his vocal appeal
jokes.explanation: Good character vanity moment contrasting self-perception with reality

jokes.text: Oh, expletive deleted! You've got a deal.
jokes.setup: Nixon being blackmailed with tape recording
jokes.punchline: Reference to his infamous transcript redactions while conceding defeat
jokes.explanation: Perfect historical callback that fits the situation and shows Nixon's habits haven't changed

jokes.text: Why'd they vote for him, now that he's just a head?
jokes.setup: Questioning why robots would vote for Nixon without his body
jokes.punchline: Sets up the reveal about robot voting patterns
jokes.explanation: Functional setup line that prepares for the final joke. Not funny on its own but serves the story.

jokes.text: Nixon has won!
jokes.setup: Exit polls showing Nixon with zero votes from humans
jokes.punchline: Robot vote completely overturns the human results
jokes.explanation: Good electoral twist showing the power of robot voters as separate constituency

jokes.text: It ain't my fault. I'm a non-voting felon. It's not my fault. I forgot to vote. Crud, I knew there was something I meant to do today.
jokes.setup: Characters disclaiming responsibility for Nixon's victory
jokes.punchline: Each excuse shows different levels of civic disengagement
jokes.explanation: Good character-consistent reasons for not voting, with escalating levels of irresponsibility

jokes.text: May death come quickly to his enemies.
jokes.setup: Morbo congratulating the new president
jokes.punchline: Typical news blessing twisted into violent threat
jokes.explanation: Perfect Morbo moment subverting traditional congratulations into menacing blessing
